title: NOC 94112 – Rubber processing machine operators and related workers | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s rubber manufacturing industry is a critical component of the automotive, aerospace, and industrial supply chains. Rubber Processing Machine Operators (NOC 94112) manage the sophisticated machinery that mixes, extrudes, and moulds rubber into essential products—from high-performance tires to specialized seals and gaskets. As the industry pivots toward sustainable "Green Rubber" and automated precision manufacturing, international workers with experience in chemical compounding and automated press operations are seeing stable demand, particularly in Ontario and Nova Scotia.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Occupations usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Processing and manufacturing machine operatorsSub-Major Group941 – Machine operators in mineral and metal processing

duties: Machine Operation: Setting up and operating machinery used for mixing, calendering, extruding, and curing rubber materials.
Compounding: Loading rubber, pigments, fillers, and chemicals into mixing machines according to precise formulas.
Assembly: Operating machines or hand tools to fit and cement rubber materials to form parts like tires or belts.
Quality Inspection: Inspecting finished products for defects and using instruments to ensure conformance to quality standards.
2026 Update: Monitoring digital sensors for real-time vulcanization data to ensure maximum durability and safety ratings.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is the standard requirement.
Experience: Previous experience as a labourer in the same company is often preferred.
Training: Several weeks of on-the-job training are typically provided by the employer.
Language: CLB 4 or 5 is generally sufficient for most entry-level provincial pathways.
Certifications: Mandatory 2026 WHMIS (Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System).
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) expects a balanced market. While automation simplifies some repetitive assembly tasks, the complexity of rubber compounding and quality testing ensures a steady need for skilled operators. Significant retirement rates in the Ontario and Nova Scotia manufacturing sectors are expected to create consistent job openings through 2033.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook for rubber machine operators is rated as Moderate to Good, with demand concentrated in Canada's industrial hubs:
Ontario: Stable demand. As Canada's automotive heartland, regions like Kitchener-Waterloo and the GTA have a consistent need for tire and rubber component operators.
Nova Scotia: Very Good prospects. The presence of major global tire manufacturers (like Michelin) makes this province a top destination for workers in this NOC.
Quebec: Good prospects. Demand is driven by specialized rubber product manufacturing for the aerospace and industrial sectors in the Montreal region.
Alberta: Stable demand. Opportunities are focused on rubber products for the energy and heavy machinery sectors.
Why Work in Canada as a Rubber Machine Operator?
Industry Stability: Rubber products are essential for transportation and infrastructure, ensuring long-term job security even during economic shifts.
Technological Integration: Work with 2026-grade technologies, including automated compounding systems, laser-guided cutting, and AI-monitored curing ovens.
Path to PR: Frequently featured in PNP In-Demand lists and eligible for the 2026 TR to PR transition streams.
Competitive Compensation: Rubber manufacturing often offers higher median wages than general light manufacturing due to the technical nature of the work.
pathways: Ontario Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): NOC 94112 is an eligible occupation for those with a valid job offer in Ontario’s industrial sectors.
Nova Scotia Labour Market Priorities: Periodic draws target skilled workers in manufacturing sectors with relevant experience.
TR to PR Pathway (2026): Rubber operators are classified as "Essential Manufacturing Workers," making them high-priority candidates for this 33,000-person transition quota.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for technical machine operators to obtain Permanent Residency with a provincial job offer.
